collisions.paper: Chanin et al. (2024). A is for Absorption: Studying Feature Splitting and Absorption in Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2409.14507
collisions.overlap: Established absorption as failure mode; proved absorption is logical consequence of sparsity loss (Proposition 2). Did not compare to random baselines or investigate metric calibration.

collisions.paper: Korznikov et al. (2025). OrtSAE: Orthogonal Sparse Autoencoders Uncover Atomic Features. arXiv:2509.22033
collisions.overlap: Reduced absorption by 65% via decoder orthogonality. Compared to standard trained SAEs but not to random baselines.

collisions.paper: Various (2026). Sanity Checks for Sparse Autoencoders: Do SAEs Beat Random Baselines? arXiv:2602.14111
collisions.overlap: Frozen/random baselines achieve comparable performance to trained SAEs on standard metrics. Does not focus on absorption specifically.

collisions.paper: Wang et al. (2026). Does Higher Interpretability Imply Better Utility? ICLR 2026. arXiv:2510.03659
collisions.overlap: Weak correlation (~0.3) between interpretability and steering utility. Consistent with our null results on absorption impact.

collisions.paper: Bussmann et al. (2025). Matryoshka SAEs. ICML 2025
collisions.overlap: Reduced absorption from 0.49 to 0.05 via nested architecture. Architectural solution to absorption.

candidates.differentiation_notes: Primary novelty: first demonstration that trained SAEs have lower absorption than random baselines (mean 0.034 vs 0.278, p<0.001). This reframes absorption as a structural artifact metric rather than learned failure. Extends the Sanity Checks challenge by focusing specifically on absorption metrics. Theoretical foundation from Chanin et al. Proposition 2 (absorption minimizes sparsity loss) but extended with empirical evidence that training reduces this artifact.
Hypothesized decoder correlation graph predicts absorption pairs. Falsified: precision@20 = 0.0. No novelty assessment needed.

collisions.paper: Various (2026). Sanity Checks for Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2602.14111
collisions.overlap: Covers random baseline comparisons at high level; methodology similar to our approach.

candidates.differentiation_notes: Pure null-result paper with methodological contributions. Limited novelty as standalone research direction. Workshop-only visibility likely. Recommend using as fallback only if cand_g framing is rejected.

key_findings: cand_g's core novelty (trained < random absorption) is genuinely novel and directly addresses literature gap #7
Chanin et al. provides theoretical foundation but does not diminish cand_g's empirical contribution
The Sanity Checks paper is the closest prior but does not focus specifically on absorption metrics
Optimal compression reframing builds on Chanin Proposition 2 but extends with training dynamics evidence (H7)
risk_factors: Field skepticism about SAE utility may depress interest regardless of novelty
The optimal compression theory is partially derivative of Chanin et al.'s Proposition 2
recommended_citations: Chanin et al. (2024) - Foundational work on absorption; must cite
Sanity Checks (2026) - Must address directly; our work extends by focusing on absorption metrics
Wang et al. ICLR 2026 - Consistent with null results; supports our methodology
Korznikov et al. OrtSAE - Architectural context; not in direct competition
assessment_notes: Web search unavailable; prior art derived from proposal.md which cites: Chanin et al. (2024), Korznikov et al. (2025), Sanity Checks (arXiv:2602.14111, 2026), Wang et al. (ICLR 2026), Matryoshka SAEs (Bussmann et al., ICML 2025). The proposal's prior art section is comprehensive and was presumably vetted in earlier iterations. No new prior art discovered since last assessment.
novelty_score_rationale: Score of 7/10 because: (1) trained < random absorption comparison is genuinely novel as specific claim about absorption metrics; (2) optimal compression reframing builds on Chanin's Proposition 2 but is not fully original; (3) honest null-result methodology is valuable but not theoretically novel.
